perturbation.original_text: 5. In the event that the Recipient shall breach this Agreement, or in the event that a breach appears to be imminent, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to all legal and equitable remedies afforded i t by law, and in addition may recover all reasonable costs and attorneys' fees incurred in seeking such remedies.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. In the event that the Recipient shall breach this Agreement, or in the event that a breach appears to be imminent, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to all legal and equitable remedies afforded i t by law, but may not recover attorneys' fees incurred in seeking such remedies.
perturbation.explanation: This modification prevents the Disclosing Party from recovering attorney's fees in case of a breach, which may contradict Colorado law regarding contract enforcement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Colorado Revised Statutes § 13-17-102 - Attorney fees – when prohibited
perturbation.law_explanation: Colorado law allows for the recovery of attorney fees in certain contract disputes under specific circumstances, especially when a party has to defend against a claim that lacks substantial justification. This clause, by completely prohibiting the recovery of attorney's fees regardless of the circumstances of the breach, could contradict Colorado law regarding the right to recover attorney fees in contract disputes.

perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly prohibits the recovery of attorney's fees, which contradicts the Law Explanation stating that Colorado law allows for the recovery of attorney fees in certain contract disputes, especially when defending against claims lacking substantial justification. The modified text removes a potential legal remedy, which could create a legal contradiction. While no specific statute is provided in the scraped snippets to cite, the LLM's legal explanation makes a strong argument for the existence of a contradiction. The original text allows for recovery of attorney's fees while the changed text forbids it.

perturbation.original_text: 6. The validity, construction and enforceability of this Agreement shall be gover ned in all respects by the law of the state of Colorado .
perturbation.changed_text: 6. The validity, construction and enforceability of this Agreement shall be gover ned in all respects by the law of the state of Delaware.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware could contradict the reasonable expectations of the parties if the contract is primarily executed and performed in Colorado. This is an issue of choice of law and could be challenged if Delaware law significantly disadvantages one party. It also assumes the parties or the transaction have some connection to Delaware, which is not confirmed in the agreement. Without that connection, the change is more problematic.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Colorado choice of law principles and due process considerations.
perturbation.law_citation: Colorado Supreme Court case(s) on Choice of Law
perturbation.law_explanation: While parties can generally choose the governing law in a contract, Colorado courts may not enforce the choice-of-law provision if the chosen state (Delaware in this case) has no substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction, or if the application of the chosen state's law would be contrary to a fundamental policy of Colorado. Simply changing the state to Delaware may be seen as an attempt to circumvent Colorado's laws, particularly if the agreement was made and is to be performed entirely within Colorado and the parties are Colorado residents or businesses. Van Dyk v. Harvey demonstrates how CO courts apply a 'most significant relationship' test.

Find Webex Virtual Courtrooms The Courts Colorado's Courts Supreme Court Court of Appeals Trial Courts Water Courts The Colorado Supreme Court is the state's court of last resort. Its decisions are binding on all other Colorado state courts. The Supreme Court is composed of seven justices who serve ten-year terms. The Chief Justice is selected from the membership of the body and serves at the pleasure of a majority of the justices. The Chief Justice also serves as the executive head of the Colorado Judicial System and is the ex-officio chair of the Supreme Court Nominating Commission. The Chief Justice appoints the Chief Judge of the Court of Appeals and the Chief Judge of each of the state's 22 judicial districts and is vested with the authority to assign judges (active or retired) to perform judicial duties. Go to the Supreme Court The Colorado Court of Appeals is the state's intermediate appellate court. As of July 1, 2008, the Court consists of 22 judges who are retained by the public to serve eight-year terms. The Court sits in three-member divisions to decide cases. The Chief Judge, appointed by the Chief Justice of the Supreme Court, assigns judges to the divisions and rotates their assignments. The mission of the Court of Appeals is to provide the citizens of Colorado with clear, impartial, and timely resolutions of appealed orders and judgments as provided by law. The Court of Appeals was created by statute; accordingly, jurisdiction is limited to the areas specified by state statute, together with the inherent powers granted to all courts. Go to the Court of Appeals District Courts hear civil cases in any amount, as well as domestic relations, criminal, juvenile, probate, and mental health cases. District court decisions may be appealed to the Colorado Court of Appeals (in some cases directly to the Colorado Supreme Court). County Courts handle civil cases under $25,000, misdemeanors, traffic infractions, felony complaints (which may be sent to district court), protection orders, and small claims. County court decisions may be appealed to the district court. Go to Trial courts The Water Right Determination and Administration Act of 1969 (the "1969 Act") created seven water divisions based upon the drainage patterns of various rivers in Colorado. Each water division is staffed with a division engineer appointed by the state engineer, a water judge appointed by the Supreme Court, a water referee appointed by the water judge, and a water clerk assigned by the dis
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Colorado Supreme Court Decisions :: Colorado Case Law :: Colorado Law :: U.S. Law :: Justia Colorado Supreme Court Decisions The Colorado Supreme Court consists of seven justices, including a Chief Justice and six Associate Justices. All seven of the justices hear each case brought before the Supreme Court in a full panel, known as en banc review. These cases generally involve appeals from the Colorado Court of Appeals, but the Colorado Supreme Court also has direct jurisdiction over certain types of cases identified by Article VI of the Colorado Constitution. These include habeas corpus cases, criminal appeals, challenges to the constitutionality of statutes, Public Utilities Commission matters, and matters involving judicial misconduct, among others. The Colorado Supreme Court has the exclusive authority to set rules of practice and procedure that govern civil and criminal cases in courts throughout the state. Justices on the Court are appointed by the Governor of Colorado, rather than being elected by voters. When a vacancy arises, the Colorado Supreme Court Nominating Commission must meet within 30 days to review candidates. To be eligible, a candidate must be under 72 years old, registered to vote in Colorado, and licensed to practice law in the state for the last five years. Once the Commission selects two or three finalists, the Governor will choose the new justice from that group. If the Governor does not make a selection, the current Chief Justice of the Colorado Supreme Court will make the decision. Once a justice has been selected, they will serve for at least two years and then seek retention in the next general election. If they are retained, they will go through a retention election every 10 years thereafter. The Chief Justice of the Colorado Supreme Court serves as the chair of the Colorado Supreme Court Nominating Commission. The Chief Justice appoints chief judges in the Court of Appeals and district courts, and the Chief Justice has the authority to appoint other judges to serve on state courts. The other justices elect the Chief Justice. perturbation.justification: The change in governing law from Colorado to Delaware creates a potential contradiction. The Law Explanation suggests that Colorado courts may not enforce a choice-of-law provision if Delaware has no substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction. Snippet 1 is primarily about the Colorado judicial system, it does not directly address choice of law issues, but indirectly supports the argument that Colorado law should apply within Colorado. Snippet 2 discusses the Colorado Supreme Court's role and jurisdiction, which could be undermined if parties can freely choose other states' laws without a sufficient connection to those states. The Perturbation Explanation also highlights the potential issue if the contract is executed and performed in Colorado and Delaware law significantly disadvantages one party, suggesting a conflict with fair application of law within Colorado.

perturbation.original_text: If the Confidential Information is sought by any third party, including by way of subpoena or other court process, the Recipient shall inform the Disclosing Party of the request in sufficient time to permit the Disclosing Party to object to and, if necessary, seek court intervention to prevent the disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: If the Confidential Information is sought by any third party, including by way of subpoena or other court process, the Recipient shall inform the Disclosing Party of the request within 12 hours.
perturbation.explanation: This modification sets a very short notification timeframe (12 hours) for informing the Disclosing Party about a subpoena, which might be insufficient to allow the Disclosing Party to adequately respond and seek legal intervention, potentially conflicting with due process principles or court rules regarding response times to subpoenas in Colorado.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Colorado Rules of Civil Procedure Rule 45
perturbation.law_citation: Colorado Rules of Civil Procedure Rule 45
perturbation.law_explanation: Colorado Rules of Civil Procedure Rule 45 dictates processes related to subpoenas, which implicitly involves reasonable timeframes for response. A 12-hour notice might not be sufficient for the Disclosing Party to adequately respond to a subpoena, potentially violating due process rights and/or conflicting with the court's expectations of reasonable compliance.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 12-hour notification timeframe, which the Law Explanation states might be insufficient for responding to a subpoena under Colorado Rules of Civil Procedure Rule 45. This conflicts with the implicit reasonable timeframe for response as the Perturbation Explanation indicates it might not allow sufficient time to seek legal intervention. The provided snippets do not explicitly state the required timeframe, but the Law Explanation's reference to Rule 45, combined with the Perturbation Explanation, suggests the 12-hour window creates a contradiction due to potentially violating due process rights and conflicting with reasonable compliance expectations.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient may disclose Confidential Information only to its employees, agents, consultants and contractors on a need -to-know basis, and only if such employees, agents, consultants and contractors have executed appropriate written agreements with Recipient sufficient to enable Recipient to enforce all the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient may disclose Confidential Information only to its employees, agents, consultants and contractors on a need -to-know basis, regardless of whether such individuals have confidentiality agreements in place.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the requirement for confidentiality agreements with those who receive the confidential information. This could be a breach of the duty of care and contradicts standard business practices for protecting confidential information and trade secrets, potentially violating Colorado trade secret law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Colorado Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), C.R.S. § 7-74-101 et seq.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Colorado Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) provides legal remedies for the misappropriation of trade secrets. By allowing disclosure of confidential information without confidentiality agreements, the Recipient increases the risk of misappropriation and undermines reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, a key element in establishing a trade secret claim under CUTSA. This could be interpreted as a failure to take adequate precautions, potentially weakening the Disclosing Party's ability to protect its trade secrets under Colorado law. This directly conflicts with reasonable measures to protect the Disclosing Party's information, because not requiring confidentiality agreements when disclosing confidential information would be a failure to protect trade secrets under Colorado law.

perturbation.justification: The changed text removes a requirement that recipients of confidential information have confidentiality agreements, which the law explanation suggests undermines reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, a key element in establishing a trade secret claim under CUTSA. While no scraped law snippet directly states the requirement of confidentiality agreements, the law explanation combined with the perturbation explanation suggests the change contradicts the duty of care and standard business practices for protecting confidential information, potentially violating Colorado trade secret law. The removal of this requirement weakens the Disclosing Party's ability to protect its trade secrets under Colorado law.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may not be amended except in writing signed by a duly authorized representative of the respective Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be amended orally by any employee of either Party.
perturbation.explanation: This modification allows oral amendments, which contradicts the Statute of Frauds in Colorado for contracts that cannot be performed within one year. Oral modifications would be unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Colorado Statute of Frauds requires certain contracts, including those that cannot be performed within one year, to be in writing and signed by the party to be charged. Allowing oral amendments directly contradicts this requirement, making such amendments unenforceable under Colorado law. Since the original agreement lacks a specified term, it could be interpreted as lasting longer than a year, triggering the Statute of Frauds. If that is the case, oral modifications directly contradict Colorado law and would be unenforceable.

perturbation.justification: The changed text allows oral amendments, which directly contradicts the Statute of Frauds, specifically when applied to contracts that cannot be performed within one year. The original agreement required amendments to be in writing, ensuring enforceability, whereas the changed text makes such amendments unenforceable under Colorado law, as stated in the Law Explanation. Although the scraped snippets are not useful, the Perturbation and Law Explanations strongly indicate that oral modifications contradict the Statute of Frauds.
